what_manipulated: severity of costly punishment option (none vs. standard vs. severe) available in a repeated exchange task
what_measured: average net payoffs and frequency of cooperative behavior
experiment: Units play a repeated public good game over many rounds in a between-subjects design with two monitoring conditions: perfect and imperfect. In perfect monitoring, all contributions and defections are observable. In imperfect monitoring, observability is limited. Punishment severity (none, standard, severe) is varied between groups. Payoffs are calculated accounting for both contribution gains and punishment costs. Mean payoffs across all rounds and subjects are compared.

what_manipulated: authority allocation (retention vs. delegation) in a coordination task with differential incentives
what_measured: choice to retain or delegate authority, effort provision, and aggregate payoffs
experiment: Units play an authority-delegation game where one unit decides whether to retain control (and set effort incentives for both) or delegate control to another unit. The delegating unit can offer a contract to the controlling unit, creating a coordination problem. Choices are observed, effort is measured, and outcomes are compared between units with authority and those without. The analysis examines whether authority is retained despite material incentives to delegate and whether authority correlates with higher effort provision.

what_manipulated: procedure for selecting an arbitrator (simultaneous veto-rank vs. sequential shortlisting)
what_measured: outcomes and efficiency of arbitrator selection, consistency with theoretical predictions
experiment: Units representing conflicting parties participate in a procedure to select an arbitrator from a candidate pool. In one condition, units simultaneously veto candidates (veto-rank); in another, units sequentially eliminate candidates (shortlisting). The selected arbitrator then rules on a hypothetical dispute. Outcomes are compared to theoretical predictions about procedure performance, focusing on whether the shortlisting procedure yields predicted efficiency advantages over veto-rank.

what_manipulated: availability of money (present vs. absent) as a medium of exchange in an economic model
what_measured: equilibrium selection (monetary vs. gift exchange), aggregate payoffs, and efficiency
experiment: Units engage in repeated exchange interactions. In one condition, money is available as a store of value and medium of exchange; in another, only barter and gift exchange are possible. Units' trading behavior and payoffs are observed over repeated rounds. The analysis determines which equilibrium emerges, whether subjects coordinate on theoretical predictions, and how welfare compares across conditions.

what_manipulated: subsidy level (none, moderate, very high) for antimalarial drugs and availability of rapid diagnostic tests over-the-counter
what_measured: treatment-seeking behavior, drug uptake, over-treatment rate, and public health outcomes
experiment: Households in Kenya are offered antimalarials at varied subsidy levels, with diagnostic testing availability manipulated independently. Purchasing behavior, whether individuals actually have malaria, and medication use are tracked. Over-treatment (taking antimalarials without malaria) is measured as a proxy for public health cost. Statistical analysis compares treatment-seeking and medication adherence across subsidy and testing conditions to assess the trade-off between access and targeting.

what_manipulated: observability of career-related preferences (exam/problem sets unobserved vs. real-stakes questionnaire observed by classmates) and observer gender (single male peers vs. others)
what_measured: participation grade, stated salary aspirations, willingness to work long hours, willingness to travel
experiment: Female MBA students take exams and problem sets where performance is unobserved, and complete a real-stakes placement questionnaire where classmates may see their preferences. Responses are compared when observability is varied. A second experiment varies the presence of single male peers during observability manipulations. Participation grades, stated salary preferences, and hours/travel willingness are measured. Analysis compares single women's responses versus married women and other groups to isolate marriage-market signaling effects.

what_manipulated: exposure to migration and cultural contact (differential access to Western democratic vs. Russian non-democratic destinations following 1998 Russian crisis)
what_measured: electoral participation, voting for non-communist parties, individual political preferences
experiment: A quasi-experimental design leverages the 1998 Russian crisis and subsequent emigration from Moldova directed to both Western Europe and Russia. Communities and individuals with greater access to Western migration destinations are compared to those with more access to Russian destinations on electoral outcomes and political preferences measured over the following decade. The analysis uses the differential direction of emigration flows as a natural experiment to identify the effect of exposure to more democratic versus less democratic political systems.

what_manipulated: individual differences in receptiveness trait; information choice context (opposing vs. own party), speech content (agreement vs. disagreement), argument type (supporting vs. opposing policy)
what_measured: trait receptiveness score, proportion of information consumed from opposing vs. aligned sources, mind wandering during speech viewing, evenhandedness of argument evaluation, news outlet selection
experiment: An 18-item self-report scale is developed to measure receptiveness to opposing views. Study 1 validates the scale's factor structure and validity. Studies 2-5 examine whether higher receptiveness scores correlate with behavior: information consumption from opposing sources, reduced mind wandering to disagreeable content, more impartial evaluation of opposing arguments, and balanced news consumption. Analyses use correlations and comparisons of high vs. low receptiveness groups across these outcome measures.

what_manipulated: institutional changes (economic liberalization and intellectual property reform); access to international resources (technology, finance)
what_measured: product market internationalization, financial performance, organizational transformation strategy
experiment: A longitudinal observational study of 206 Indian pharmaceutical firms over 1995-2004 tracks how institutional discontinuities affect organizational strategies. Firms' access to international technological and financial resources is measured and correlated with internationalization of product markets. Time period and business group affiliation are examined as moderators. Financial performance is compared across firms with differing resource access and internationalization levels. Regression and conditional analyses assess whether international resource access predicts market internationalization and whether internationalization predicts financial performance.

what_manipulated: presence of a healthy food item (absent vs. present) paired with an unhealthy main item; presence of a reference food standard; order of viewing (food presented alone vs. with reference)
what_measured: estimated calorie content of the unhealthy food item
experiment: Units are shown images of foods and asked to estimate calorie content. In Study 1-2, unhealthy foods are presented alone or alongside healthy foods; in Study 3, a reference food is presented before judging a test food. Calorie estimates and healthiness ratings are recorded. Analyses compare estimates across presence of healthy items and order of presentation. The study tests whether healthy foods cause an averaging effect or a contrast/anchoring effect on calorie estimates.

what_manipulated: task complexity (varying depth of Theory-of-Mind reasoning required in reference games); none (observational of individual differences)
what_measured: pragmatic reasoning depth, comprehension accuracy, referential success
experiment: Units play reference games requiring listeners to infer a speaker's intended referent from pragmatic cues. Games vary in the cognitive depth required (level 0, 1, or 2 reasoning about speaker intent). Success rates and response patterns are modeled using Bayesian hierarchical models assuming either homogeneous or heterogeneous populations. Model comparisons via Bayes factor determine whether population-level heterogeneity in reasoning depth improves prediction of individual behavior.

what_manipulated: opportunity and salience of dishonest behavior; moral standard salience (present vs. absent or inattentional); categorization flexibility (behavior labeled honestly vs. dishonestly)
what_measured: extent of dishonest action taken, profit gained, self-rated integrity/honesty
experiment: Units complete tasks where dishonest behavior yields monetary profit. Six experiments manipulate the availability of dishonesty, the salience of moral standards, and how dishonest acts can be categorized. Dishonesty is measured as the degree to which subjects behave dishonestly. Self-reported integrity is assessed post-hoc. Analyses compare dishonesty levels across manipulations and test whether inattention to morality and flexible categorization explain behavior, supporting a theory that people behave dishonestly while maintaining positive self-views.

what_manipulated: surface material of display table (varied textures); self-view salience (interdependent vs. independent self-concept prime)
what_measured: product evaluation ratings relative to table surface associations
experiment: Units view products displayed on tables with different surface materials and rate the products. Self-view is primed (interdependent vs. independent cognition) to vary holistic vs. analytic processing. The association between table material and product category is established separately. Evaluation ratings are compared to expected associations with table materials. Analysis tests for assimilation (evaluations aligned with table associations) vs. contrast (evaluations opposite to associations) as moderated by self-view priming.

what_manipulated: presence of counterfeit luxury brand option; luxury brand attitude function (social-adjustive vs. value-expressive); moral beliefs (salience or strength of moral concerns about counterfeits)
what_measured: preference for counterfeit brand, change in preference for real brand, moral judgment influence
experiment: Units indicate their attitudes toward luxury brands and the functions those attitudes serve (fitting in vs. expressing values). Counterfeit versions are introduced, and preference is measured. The influence of moral beliefs on counterfeit preference is examined. Analyses show that the relationship between attitude function and counterfeit preference is moderated by attitude function type, and that moral beliefs affect counterfeit preference differentially depending on the function served by luxury brand attitudes.

what_manipulated: product brand presence (category-level vs. branded variant); product category (typically utilitarian vs. typically symbolic in attitude function)
what_measured: attitude function classification (utilitarian vs. symbolic), persuasiveness of matched vs. mismatched appeal type
experiment: Attitudes toward product categories are first characterized as serving primarily utilitarian or symbolic functions. Branded vs. unbranded versions of these products are then evaluated to assess attitude function shifts. Persuasion is tested by exposing units to appeals matched or mismatched to the attitude function. Ratings and preference measures are compared to determine whether branding alters attitude functions and whether the function-matching advantage in persuasion holds at brand vs. category level.

what_manipulated: decision autonomy (independent decision-making vs. delegating to surrogate); opportunity for self-affirmation (free will affirmation opportunity present vs. absent); memory cue (recalling delegated vs. independent past decision)
what_measured: self-regulatory resource depletion (measured via subsequent self-control task performance), decision choice, and self-control impairment
experiment: Units make real decisions (e.g., financial or health-related) either independently or by delegating to a surrogate. Subsequent self-control is measured via a task requiring impulse resistance. Study 1 compares independent vs. delegated decisions on self-control depletion. Study 2 tests whether affirming free will reduces depletion from delegation. Studies 3-4 measure recall of past delegated vs. independent decisions and examine effects on current self-control. Analyses use self-control task performance as the dependent variable.

what_manipulated: framing of a risky prospect (labeled as lottery/raffle/coin flip/gamble vs. gift certificate/voucher); risk level (varied); possible outcome values (varied across experiments)
what_measured: willingness to pay for the prospect, willingness to accept a prospect, and the magnitude of discrepancy between the two
experiment: Units make real and hypothetical purchasing decisions on risky prospects (gambles) that are framed using different labels. In Study 1, willingness to pay is compared across frames. Studies 2-3 additionally measure willingness to accept. Study 4 varies objective uncertainty. Analyses test whether frame influences price differently for buyers vs. sellers and whether the effect depends on the perceived risk association of the frame label or the prospect's outcome values.

what_manipulated: static logo design (varied to evoke different degrees of perceived movement); congruence between perceived movement and brand characteristics (congruent vs. incongruent)
what_measured: perceived dynamic imagery from static logo, consumer engagement (self-report and eye-tracking), brand attitude
experiment: Units view brand logos and rate perceived movement (dynamic imagery). Engagement is measured via self-report and eye-tracking technology (e.g., gaze duration, fixation patterns). Brand attitude is assessed via rating scales. The relationship between perceived movement and engagement is estimated. Moderation by congruence between movement type and brand characteristics is tested. Path analysis or regression examines the chain: movement → engagement → attitude.

what_manipulated: salience of scarcity (abundant availability vs. scarce availability) of a product class; arousal state (manipulated directly in one experiment)
what_measured: choice share of most-preferred vs. non-preferred items in the product class, polarization of preference ratings
experiment: Units are made aware of abundant or scarce availability of a product category, then choose from multiple items in that category or rate the items. Five experiments measure choice share of the favorite item and evaluate ratings of favorites vs. non-favorites. One experiment directly manipulates arousal to test mediation. Analyses test whether scarcity increases choice share of the most-preferred item by polarizing evaluations through heightened arousal.

what_manipulated: goal conflict perception (tasks designed to vary perceived conflict between goals); stress and anxiety (measured and manipulated); intervention type (slow breathing vs. anxiety reappraisal vs. control)
what_measured: subjective time pressure (feeling of time scarcity), time allocation, willingness to pay to save time, stress, and anxiety
experiment: Five experiments present units with multiple competing goals that vary in perceived conflict (related vs. unrelated to time demands). Perceived time scarcity and stress/anxiety are measured. Some experiments manipulate goal conflict framing or offer interventions (breathing, reappraisal). Time allocation choices and willingness-to-pay for time-saving services are measured. Mediation analysis tests whether stress and anxiety explain the effect of goal conflict on time scarcity perception.
